topicTitle: Topic 1: Reading Tables, Graphs, and Scatterplots

value.text: The SAT often presents data in
tables, bar graphs, line graphs, or scatterplots

and asks you to interpret or compare values.  
Your task is to
, identify patterns, and connect the question to what’s shown.
Show exact numerical values, often for multiple categories.
Show trends or change over time.
Show relationships between two variables. Look for positive, negative, or no correlation.
Identify which variable is on each axis.
Read exact values (e.g., from a table or bar height).
Estimate trends and slopes for graphs.
Recognize outliers and general patterns in scatterplots.
A table lists the number of books sold by a store each month:
The number of books increases by 30 each month.
How many books were sold in March?

A bar graph (insert here: “bar graph showing the number of students choosing subjects Math, Science, English, History, and Art”)  
shows the tallest bar at Science with 50 students, and the shortest bar at Art with 20 students.

A line graph (insert here: “line graph showing average temperature over 12 months”)  
shows a steady rise from January to July, peaking at 85°F, then declining.
During which months does temperature increase the fastest?

A scatterplot (insert here: “scatterplot of hours studied vs. SAT Math score”)  
shows points roughly following an upward trend.
As hours studied increase, SAT scores tend to increase — this is a

In the same scatterplot, one point shows a student who studied 20 hours but scored very low.  
This point is an
, meaning it does not fit the general pattern.

Correlation does not imply causation.  
Even if two variables move together, one does not necessarily cause the other (for example, ice cream sales and drowning incidents both increase in summer, but one does not cause the other).
Identify variable types and axes before answering.
Use the graph or table for exact or approximate values as needed.
Check for overall trends, correlations, and anomalies.
Be cautious when interpreting correlations — do not assume cause and effect.
The table below shows sales for a company over six months.
What is the total sales for the six months?
Using the same table, what is the percent increase in sales from January to June?

A new employee earns the following weekly salaries (in dollars): 500, 520, 540, 560, 580.  
  What is the average weekly salary?
What is the percent increase from 2018 to 2019?

In a survey, 40 students chose their favorite sport: 10 basketball, 15 soccer, 8 baseball, 7 swimming.  
  What percent chose soccer?

topicTitle: Topic 2: Mean, Median, Mode, and Range

value.text: is even, average of the two middle values.
. Median is more resistant to extreme values.
Sort the data before finding the median and range.
Compute mean quickly using totals or by using balance reasoning.
Identify modes from a list or simple frequency table.
Decide which measure best represents the center given outliers or skew.

.  
The 100 raises the mean above the median. Median better represents the typical score.

(highest frequency).  
Sorted positions 4th and 5th are both 4, so median
If each data value increases by

, range unchanged.  
If each data value is multiplied by
, mean, median, and range are multiplied by
Use the median when data are skewed or have outliers.
Mean equals total divided by count. Keep track of
Mode describes most common value. There can be more than one or none.
Range measures spread but is sensitive to extremes.

The ages of employees in a small company are: 22, 25, 25, 26, 27, 29, 60.  
  Which measure (mean or median) better represents the typical age? Explain.
Median better. The 60 is an outlier that pulls the mean up.
A dataset has mean 50 and median 48. Is the distribution likely skewed left, right, or symmetric?
A dataset of scores: 75, 80, 82, 83, 85, 90, 100. Identify the outlier and describe its effect on the mean.
. It increases the mean more than the median.
If 5 is added to every number in a dataset, how do the mean and median change?
Mean increases by 5. Median increases by 5.
If every value in a dataset is doubled, what happens to the mean, median, and range?
Mean and median both double. Range doubles.
. Median is average of 5th and 6th values, both 4, so median
Find the mean and identify the mode.
. Median is average of 4th and 5th values, both 2, so median
. Median is between 9th and 10th values; both are 80, so median
Find the most common number of pets (mode) and the mean.
The mean of five numbers is 12. Four of the numbers are 8, 10, 14, and 18. Find the fifth number.
The average of 6 test scores is 80. If one score (60) is dropped, what is the average of the remaining 5 scores?
A class of 10 students has an average height of 160 cm. If one new student with height 180 cm joins, what is the new mean height?
The median of 7 numbers is 10. If the largest number increases by 20, what happens to the median?
The median is the 4th of 7 numbers. Changing the largest leaves the median
A dataset has mean 40 and range 12. If each value increases by 3, what are the new mean and range?
A student’s five quiz scores are 85, 90, 80, 100, and 75. If the lowest score is dropped, what is the mean of the remaining scores?
The mean of four numbers is 25. When a fifth number is added, the new mean becomes 30. What is the fifth number?
The median of a set of 9 numbers is 50. If the largest value increases by 10, what happens to the median?
Median is the 5th value. Increasing largest does not change it.
In a dataset, the mode is 8 and the range is 12. If all values are doubled, what are the new mode and range?
The mean of three numbers is 20. Two of the numbers are 15 and 25. Find the third number.

topicTitle: Topic 3: Weighted Averages and Combined Means

value.text: gives different importance (weights) to each data value.
Weights may represent frequency, percentage, or relative importance.
When combining groups with different averages, use the formula:
Multiply each value by its corresponding weight.
Divide by the sum of weights to find the weighted mean.
For group combinations, use total sums or averages with counts.
Recognize that a weighted average leans toward the group with the larger weight.

A student has grades:  
Homework 90 (weight 40%), Tests 80 (weight 60%).

Group A: average 70, 10 people.  
Group B: average 90, 20 people.
A student’s grade is 85 on homework (20%), 90 on quizzes (30%), and 80 on tests (50%).

Two factories produce widgets. Factory A makes 500 widgets with average cost $10.  
Factory B makes 200 widgets with average cost $12.
Weighted average = total of “value × weight” ÷ total weight.
Combined mean depends on both averages and group sizes.
Greater weight pulls the overall mean closer to that group’s mean.
Useful for class averages, grade calculations, and mixture problems.
A grade is based on 40% homework (average 80) and 60% tests (average 90). Find the overall average.
A quiz is worth 25% of a student’s grade and the final exam 75%. If the quiz score is 88 and the exam score is 76, find the final grade.
A student’s grades are: 85 (10%), 75 (30%), 95 (60%). Find the weighted average.
An investor earns 4% on $2000 and 8% on $3000. Find the overall rate of return.
Class A: 20 students, average 70. Class B: 30 students, average 80. Find the combined average.
Two sets of data have averages 40 and 60 with 5 and 15 items respectively. Find the combined mean.
The mean of Group X (50 people) is 120. The mean of Group Y (150 people) is 130. Find the combined mean.
A factory produces 300 units at $10 each and 200 units at $15 each. Find the average unit price.
Group 1: average 75, size 12; Group 2: average 90, size 8. Find combined mean.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part C: Frequency and Percent Data

value.text: A student’s semester grade is based on Homework 20%, Tests 50%, and Final 30%. Scores are 92, 84, and 78 respectively. Find the weighted average.
A car travels 40 km at 60 km/h and 60 km at 40 km/h. Find its average speed for the trip.
A business has two departments: Dept A (60 employees, avg salary $50,000), Dept B (40 employees, avg salary $70,000). Find the overall average salary.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part D: Conceptual and SAT-Style Problems

value.text: The mean of a data set is 20. If another data set of equal size has mean 40, what is the mean when they are combined?
The mean of 10 numbers is 12. Another 20 numbers have mean 18. What is the combined mean?
If one test counts twice as much as another, how would you compute the weighted mean of the two test scores?
If one test counts twice: weighted mean
Two classes have the same mean test score. One has twice as many students. If they are combined, what happens to the overall mean?
A student’s scores are 80, 90, 100, weighted 1, 2, 3 respectively. Find the weighted average.
measures how likely an event is to occur:

(certain).  
For independent events, probabilities multiply.  
For mutually exclusive events, probabilities add.

value.text: Compute simple probabilities for dice, cards, and spinners.
Identify whether events are independent or dependent.
Use complementary probability for “at least one” problems.
Flip a coin and roll a die.
A box has 3 red and 2 blue balls. Draw 2 without replacement.
A coin is flipped twice. The possible outcomes are HH, HT, TH, TT.
Use complements for “at least” or “none” types.
For dependent events, adjust denominators as items are removed.
All probabilities must sum to 1 for the entire sample space.

measures the chance of an event given that another event has occurred.
Use two way tables to compute conditional probabilities by dividing the appropriate cell by the given row or column total.
Use tree diagrams with branch probabilities to track multi step processes.

value.text: A survey records whether students prefer tea or coffee and whether they are juniors or seniors.

.  
Not equal, so Tea preference and Senior status are not independent.

A test is positive with probability 0.9 if a person has a condition and 0.1 if not. Prevalence is 5%.
Interpretation: despite a high true positive rate, the low prevalence means a positive result is only about 32.1% likely to indicate the condition.

From a standard deck, one card is drawn and observed to be a face card. What is the probability it is a heart?  
There are 12 face cards total and 3 are hearts.
For tables, divide the intersection cell by the given condition total.
For sequential processes, multiply along branches and add across paths.
Independence means conditioning does not change the probability.

topicTitle: Topic 6: Understanding Variability and Standard Deviation (Conceptual)

value.text: measures how spread out data values are. Two data sets can have the same mean but very different variability.
(max–min) is a simple measure of spread.
measures the average distance of data points from the mean.
A small SD means data are close to the mean; a large SD means they are widely spread.
The formula for the population standard deviation is:
In this unit, focus on interpreting—not calculating—standard deviation.
Recognize what larger or smaller standard deviation indicates.
Compare two data sets to decide which has greater spread.
Understand that adding or multiplying all data values affects the standard deviation:
Interpret SD in the context of SAT word problems and graphs.
Both have mean 6, but Set A is more spread out. So Set A has a larger SD.
Set Y’s mean is higher and its SD much larger because of the outlier 20.
Insert placeholder graph: two bell-shaped curves centered at the same mean; one narrow, one wide.
The wider curve has greater SD because its values are more spread from the mean.

If every test score increases by 5 points, SD is unchanged.  
If every score doubles, SD doubles.

Set A: daily high temperatures (degrees F) over one week in a tropical climate.  
Set B: daily highs in a northern climate with big changes.  
Set B has higher SD because of greater temperature variation.
Adding constants shifts data, not spread.
Multiplying constants changes the spread proportionally.
What does the standard deviation of a data set measure?
SD measures typical distance of data values from the mean; it quantifies spread.
If two data sets have the same mean but different standard deviations, what does that tell you?
Same mean, different SD means one set is more spread out than the other.
How does adding a constant (like +5) to all values affect the SD?
Adding a constant shifts all values equally, SD is unchanged.
How does multiplying all data values by 3 affect the SD?
Multiplying by 3 scales all distances from the mean by 3, SD triples.
What effect does an outlier have on SD?
An outlier increases SD because it adds large squared deviations from the mean.
Set A has larger SD. Its values are farther from the mean than B’s clustered values.
Set Y has greater variability. X has SD 0, Y has positive SD.
. How does SD of Q compare to SD of P?
SD of Q is 10 times SD of P, since Q is P multiplied by 10.
Two classes took the same quiz. Class 1’s scores are tightly grouped around the mean; Class 2’s are spread widely. Which class has a higher SD?
Class 2 has higher SD due to wider spread.

Which of these is likely to have the smallest SD:  
  (a) Heights of adult men, (b) Daily temperatures in one city over a week, (c) Scores on a random guessing quiz?
Smallest SD: heights of adult men. The other contexts vary more from trial to trial.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part C: Interpreting Graphs and Scenarios

value.text: Insert placeholder: two bell curves with same mean; Curve A is narrower, Curve B is wider.
Which has the larger SD, and why?
Curve B (wider) has larger SD because values are more dispersed from the mean.
A histogram shows that most data values are close to the mean. What does that say about SD?
Most values near the mean implies a small SD.
Two data sets each have mean 50. One has SD = 2, the other SD = 10. Which is more consistent?
The set with SD 2 is more consistent; values cluster tightly around 50.
If the SD of a sample is 0, what does that mean about the data?
SD 0 means all data values are identical.
Why does the presence of an extreme value (outlier) increase the SD?
Because SD uses squared deviations, a single extreme value contributes a large term, raising SD.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part D: Real-World and SAT-Style Problems

value.text: Test A: scores clustered around 75; Test B: scores from 40 to 100. Which test shows more consistency?
Test A shows more consistency; scores cluster around 75, so smaller SD.
A class’s quiz scores have mean 80, SD 5. Another class’s scores have mean 80, SD 15. Which class’s performance was more consistent?
The class with SD 5 is more consistent; the class with SD 15 is more variable.
The mean weekly income for two towns is the same, but Town A’s SD is higher. What does that imply?
Higher SD in Town A implies incomes are more spread out around the mean in Town A.
Two SAT math sections have similar averages, but one has a much larger SD. What does that say about the range of student abilities?
Larger SD indicates a wider range of abilities or performance on that section.
If all students’ SAT scores increase by 100 points next year, what happens to the SD of scores?
Adding the same 100 to all scores shifts the mean up by 100 but leaves SD unchanged.

topicTitle: Topic 7: Interpreting Trend Lines and Regression (Conceptual)

value.text:
(or line of best fit) shows the general direction of data in a scatterplot.  
It helps predict values and understand the relationship between two variables.
as one variable increases, the other tends to increase.
as one variable increases, the other tends to decrease.
A line of best fit is often written as:
is the slope (rate of change) and
Identify direction (positive, negative, none) of correlation.
Interpret slope and intercept in real-world context.
Use the line to make predictions or estimates.
Recognize that correlation does not imply causation.
Insert placeholder: scatterplot showing upward trend between hours studied and test score.

As study hours increase, scores increase.  
The trend line shows a positive slope.
Insert placeholder: scatterplot showing downward trend between absences and GPA.

As absences increase, GPA decreases.  
Negative slope, negative correlation.
Insert placeholder: random scatter with no clear pattern.
No consistent relationship between the two variables.

examples.title: Example 4: Interpreting the Equation of a Trend Line

value.text: : each additional study hour increases score by about 5 points.
: if 0 hours studied, predicted score is 60.
measures strength and direction of a linear relationship:

Even with strong correlation, one variable may not cause the other.  
Example: Ice cream sales and drownings both rise in summer—correlated due to a third factor (temperature).
Trend lines describe relationships and allow prediction.
The slope shows rate of change; the intercept gives baseline prediction.
What does a positive correlation between two variables mean?
What does a negative correlation mean?
What does it mean if there is no correlation?
Can two variables be strongly correlated without one causing the other? Explain.
Yes. A third variable can drive both or it can be coincidence. Correlation does not imply causation.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part C: Interpreting Equations of Trend Lines

value.text: Slope 4 means each 1 unit increase in
Interpret the intercept in that same line.
models GPA vs. absences. What does the slope mean here?
Each additional absence decreases predicted GPA by 2 points.

practiceParts.partTitle: Part D: Correlation Coefficient and Strength

value.text: Which of the following shows the weakest linear relationship:
, what does that tell you about the data?
Perfect negative linear relationship. All points on a line with negative slope.
Which of the following is most likely for the relationship between height and weight:
is most plausible. Height and weight are positively related.
A trend line for the relationship between hours studied (
. Predict the score for a student who studies 4 hours.
represents the relationship between number of absences and test score. Interpret the slope.
Each additional absence lowers predicted score by 3 points.

Which situation is likely to have a negative correlation?  
  (a) Hours of sleep vs. alertness  
  (b) Distance driven vs. gas left  
  (c) Hours studied vs. test score
Why does a strong correlation not always mean one variable causes the other?
There may be lurking variables or coincidence. Direction and strength do not establish cause.
